text.title: Evidence Hierarchy and Interview Framework
text.summary: The 5-level validation evidence hierarchy, commitment signal types, red flags that indicate weak evidence, and the structured interview opener.

rule.statement: Rank all evidence using the 5-level hierarchy and declare a problem validated only when at least 3 tier-1 or tier-2 signals come from cold, non-network respondents.
rule.rationale: Excitement and verbal interest are cheap. Payment and behavioral commitment are costly signals that indicate genuine motivation. Levels 4-5 are easily faked; agents that weight all five levels equally produce false positives.

list.item: Level 1 (strongest): Users paid for a solution (actual behavior).
Level 2: Users signed up or made a commitment (letter of intent, pre-order, time investment).
Level 3: Users engaged deeply with a prototype.
Level 4: Users expressed strong interest (stated preference).
Level 5 (weakest): Users said they have the problem.
Time: agreed to a follow-up meeting; used the prototype.
Reputation: introduced you to their boss or team; referred you to a colleague.
Money: letter of intent with exec sign-off; pre-order payment.

rule.statement: Treat commitment signals as strong only when backed by a concrete past action; "I would definitely introduce you to my team" is level 4, not level 2.
rule.rationale: Letters of intent without executive sign-off are forgeable. Future-tense commitments ("I would") are level-4 interest, not level-2 commitment.

rule.statement: Treat compliments, hypotheticals, and generic statements as red flags that indicate weak or fabricated signal.
rule.rationale: Friends compliment. Hypotheticals ("I would definitely...") are costless to give and carry no predictive value for actual purchase behavior.

list.item: Compliments: "Great idea!", "That's brilliant!", "I love this concept."
Hypotheticals: "I would definitely use this", "I'd probably pay for that."
Generics: "Everyone needs this", "Nobody does this well."
Approval-fishing: answering what they think you want to hear.

rule.statement: Open every validation interview with the 5-step Vision-Framing-Weakness-Pedestal-Ask pattern; agents that skip the Weakness step produce performance responses.
rule.rationale: Stating weakness (admitting you might be wrong and want honest feedback) creates psychological safety for the respondent to give critical answers instead of compliments.

list.item: Vision: state the problem you want to solve.
Framing: explain you have nothing to sell — you are just learning.
Weakness: humbly acknowledge you might be wrong about the problem.
Pedestal: emphasize their experience is the expert input you need.
Ask: request their time and honest experience.
